stories.headline: Aspen Acres Fire Becomes One of Largest in Colorado History as Evacuations Expand to Colorado City and Wetmore
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire consumed an additional 27 square miles overnight and into Friday, reaching 73,366 acres with zero containment and becoming the eighth-largest wildfire in Colorado history. The Custer County Sheriff's Office ordered mandatory evacuations for the Wetmore area, while Pueblo County ordered mandatory evacuations for Colorado City, including areas west of Interstate 25. More than 10,000 people are now under mandatory evacuation orders across Pueblo and Custer counties.

Firefighters reported extreme fire behavior, with flames running into Colorado City, up Table Mountain, and toward Bondurant Road. Smoke reduced visibility along I-25 to as little as half a mile, and sections of Lake Pueblo were closed to boaters so aircraft could scoop water for firefighting operations. Incident command officials noted that the overnight humidity recovery that typically moderates fire behavior did not occur until near dawn.

The Aspen Acres fire is one of five major fires burning across Colorado, joining the Gold Mountain, Snyder, Ferris, and Willow fires in a combined footprint exceeding 157,652 acres — more than 246 square miles. Emergency managers in southwest Colorado also ordered additional evacuations for the Ferris fire after activity picked up Friday afternoon east of Montezuma County Road 16.

Red Flag Warnings remain in effect across much of the state, with relative humidity dropping as low as 4 percent and wind gusts reaching 40 mph. Forecasters expect the hot, dry, and windy pattern to persist through the holiday weekend, with only a slight chance of isolated afternoon thunderstorms that could produce dry lightning and spark new fire starts.

stories.headline: Man Drowns at Chatfield Reservoir After Inner Tube Capsizes with Three Children Aboard
stories.summary:
William Macaluso, 48, drowned Thursday evening at Chatfield Reservoir after the inner tube he was sharing with three children capsized. The children were safely recovered with the help of family members and nearby boaters, but Macaluso went under and did not resurface. Police described him as an experienced swimmer who was not wearing a life jacket at the time of the incident.

Multiple agencies responded to the search, including South Metro Fire, West Metro Fire, and Colorado Parks and Wildlife officers, who deployed rescue boats and divers. Authorities reported Macaluso missing around 5:45 p.m. and transitioned the mission from rescue to recovery at approximately 7:30 p.m. The search was suspended around 9 p.m. due to darkness.

The Jefferson County Sheriff's Office confirmed that Macaluso's body was recovered shortly before noon on Friday. The incident remains under investigation. Officials did not release information about the three children beyond confirming they were safely recovered.

stories.headline: Update: Victor Marx Holds Narrow Lead Over Kirkmeyer in Colorado Republican Governor's Race
stories.summary:
Victor Marx has maintained a steady lead over state Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer in the Republican primary for governor, with unofficial results showing Marx at 206,330 votes to Kirkmeyer's approximately 204,310 out of roughly 518,051 votes cast. Marx, a ministry leader, overtook Kirkmeyer on Wednesday evening after trailing her by as many as 8,000 votes on election night. The race remains too close to call, with less than one percentage point separating the two candidates.

Scott Bottoms, the third candidate in the race, remained in a distant third place. The winner will face Democrat Phil Weiser, the state's current attorney general, who defeated U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet in the Democratic primary by nearly nine points. The general election contest will determine who succeeds term-limited Democratic Gov. Jared Polis.

Colorado has elected only one Republican governor in the past 50 years, making the outcome of the GOP primary race particularly significant for the party's strategy heading into November.

stories.headline: Republicans Move to Tie All Colorado Democrats to Democratic Socialist Melat Kiros After Primary Upset
stories.summary:
Within hours of Tuesday's primary results, national and state Republicans launched a coordinated strategy of linking Colorado Democratic candidates to Melat Kiros, the 29-year-old democratic socialist who defeated 15-term incumbent Rep. Diana DeGette in the state's 1st Congressional District. RNC Chairman Joe Gruters called Kiros and other progressive winners 'full-blown crazies' and said their victories proved there was 'no room for moderates' in the Democratic Party. U.S. Rep. Lauren Boebert called Kiros' win a 'gift to Republicans.'

The strategy poses a particular challenge for Democrats running in competitive districts, such as state Rep. Manny Rutinel, the Democratic nominee in the tossup 8th Congressional District. Political observers note that Kiros is expected to win easily in Denver's deep-blue 1st District in November but that her progressive politics could be used to paint more moderate Democrats in swing areas as out of step with mainstream voters.

Kiros and Colorado Democratic Party Chair Shad Murib sought to frame the primary results as a sign of a healthy, diverse party rather than an ideological fracture. Kiros argued that Democrats share common goals even if they differ on methods, while Murib pointed to the party's ability to nominate figures as different as Sen. John Hickenlooper and Kiros as evidence of strength. However, some Democrats at a post-primary unity event expressed private concerns about the electoral implications of the progressive surge.

stories.headline: Denver's Democratic Socialist Movement Gains Momentum Following Primary Victories
stories.summary:
The electoral victories of democratic socialist-aligned candidates in Tuesday's Colorado primary elections reflect years of organizing by Denver's chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America, which has grown to roughly 1,800 members since its founding in 2017. Melat Kiros' defeat of Rep. Diana DeGette was the most prominent result, but DSA-backed or aligned candidates have also won seats on Denver City Council and in Aurora in recent election cycles. The national DSA reports more than 100,000 members, with chapters in all 50 states.

DSA national committee member Andrew Thompson attributed the movement's recent growth to frustration with establishment Democrats' response to the Trump administration, arguing the party has failed to offer a coherent opposition vision. The Denver chapter has endorsed candidates including sitting councilmembers Sarah Parady and Shontel Lewis, as well as mayoral candidate Lisa Calderón and several other local officeholders. DSA-affiliated organizations have also received significant financial contributions routed through local political groups.

Critics of the movement argue that democratic socialist candidates risk alienating moderate and conservative-leaning voters in competitive districts, potentially costing Democrats winnable races in November. Supporters counter that worker-focused economic policies address widening inequality in ways that resonate with a growing share of the electorate, particularly younger voters in urban areas.

stories.headline: PNC Bank Executive Acknowledges Rocky FirstBank Integration as About 15,000 Accounts Reported Problems
stories.summary:
PNC Bank's Colorado Regional President Kevin Classen, the former CEO of Lakewood-based FirstBank, acknowledged customer difficulties during the late-June technology conversion that merged FirstBank's accounts into PNC's systems. Approximately 1.9 percent of accounts — roughly 15,000 out of 780,000 — reported issues during the transition, which included long branch lines, ATMs running out of cash, extended customer service hold times, and problems with online access and debit card activation. PNC's parent company paid $4.1 billion to acquire FirstBank.

Classen said the bank deployed more than 600 personnel from across the country to assist local staff at 95 branches in Colorado and Arizona and at customer support centers. The actual data transfer went smoothly, he said, and no accounts or data were lost. Many of the problems stemmed from customers' unfamiliarity with PNC's digital platforms and security verification processes rather than technical failures.

As of the end of June, operations were returning to normal, with customer service wait times averaging about one minute and branch traffic nearly back to pre-conversion levels. Some customers continued to deal with lingering issues, including third-party vendors that had not yet updated their records with new account information. FirstBank had been Colorado's second-largest bank by deposits before the acquisition.

stories.headline: Little Man Ice Cream Owner Converting Historic Highland Church Into Wellness Hub
stories.summary:
Paul Tamburello, owner of the well-known Little Man Ice Cream brand, is converting a historic Denver church in the Highland neighborhood into a wellness-focused mixed-use building. The planned tenants include fitness, spa, and coffee businesses. Details on a timeline for the project's opening were not disclosed.

Tamburello's venture represents another adaptive reuse project in one of Denver's most active neighborhoods for development and redevelopment. The Highland area has seen significant commercial and residential activity in recent years as property values have climbed and new concepts seek distinctive spaces.

stories.headline: Parker Approves Pick N Tap Entertainment Venue with Pickleball, Golf Simulators and Restaurant
stories.summary:
The Town of Parker has approved a proposal by Pick N Tap Co. to develop a multi-concept entertainment venue on a 2.83-acre property purchased for $1.5 million near EchoPark Stadium. The venue will feature a restaurant, pickleball courts, and golf simulators. The project is among several entertainment-focused developments targeting suburban Denver markets.

The Parker location near EchoPark Stadium positions the venue to attract both sports event traffic and local residents seeking entertainment options. Pickleball-anchored venues have expanded rapidly across the Denver metro in recent years as the sport's popularity has surged among a wide range of age groups.

stories.headline: Denver Leads Nation in 'Accidental Landlords' as 5% of Former Home Listings Convert to Rentals
stories.summary:
Denver tops every major metro area in the country for the share of homes that were listed for sale and then converted to rentals, at 5 percent — more than double the national average of roughly 2 percent, according to Zillow research. The trend is being driven largely by homeowners who purchased at low interest rates and are reluctant to sell in a slower market while also unwilling to take on a new mortgage at today's higher rates. Industry observers have termed this group 'accidental landlords.'

Seamus Nally, CEO of Colorado-based rental platform TurboTenant, said the economics often work in homeowners' favor, with landlords able to pocket $500 to $1,000 per month after covering their existing mortgage through rental income. That gap can help offset the cost of purchasing a new home. Denver homeowner Megan Cuellar described listing her north Denver home in summer 2025, watching it sit for six months, and ultimately renting it for $3,500 a month while purchasing a larger home in Conifer.

First-time landlords face regulatory requirements that can catch them off guard, including Denver's mandatory rental license and local rules governing tenant screening, security deposits, and late fees. Nally said most new landlords find the process more manageable than expected once they establish systems for screening and documentation.

stories.headline: Nuggets Sign Marvin Bagley III as LeBron James Pursuit Intensifies During NBA Free Agency
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets made their first roster addition of the 2026 offseason by signing eight-year veteran Marvin Bagley III, according to Denver7. The signing came as the NBA free agency period opened and the Nuggets simultaneously engaged in discussions with LeBron James, who has listed Denver as one of five serious destinations alongside Cleveland, Philadelphia, Miami, and Minnesota.

James' agent Rich Paul confirmed his client's interest in the Nuggets on a podcast appearance, using a whiteboard to identify the Nuggets as a legitimate suitor alongside four other franchises. Paul cited Nikola Jokic, Jamal Murray, Cam Johnson, and Aaron Gordon as an attractive supporting cast for James, who is entering what is widely expected to be his final NBA season. James is said to be prioritizing winning and happiness over maximum salary in his next contract.

Denver Post columnist Nick Renck argued that Nuggets ownership — Stan and Josh Kroenke — must pursue James aggressively, even if it means absorbing significant luxury tax penalties by going into the second apron. The Kroenkes and James have an existing relationship, and James is said to respect KSE's ownership record across multiple championship franchises. The Nuggets have not won a playoff series in three consecutive seasons following their 2023 NBA championship.

stories.headline: USA Rugby Nations Cup Match at Dick's Sporting Goods Park Doubles as Denver's 2031 World Cup Audition
stories.summary:
The United States Men's Eagles face Portugal in a Nations Cup match at Dick's Sporting Goods Park in Commerce City on July 4, a game organizers and players alike are framing as an important showcase for Denver's bid to host the 2031 Rugby World Cup and 2033 Women's Rugby World Cup. Denver has a long history with the sport, anchored by Glendale's designation as 'Rugbytown USA' and Infinity Park, the first rugby-specific stadium in the United States. The Denver Onyx, Colorado's pro women's team, are the reigning Women's Elite Rugby champions.

National team captain Jason Damm, whose professional career included stints with a Vail-based team and the now-defunct Glendale Raptors, called Dick's Sporting Goods Park a meaningful venue for connecting the sport with a national audience. Two Colorado-connected players — prop Kaleb Geiger of Castle View High School and lock Sam Golla of Denver East High School — are in the national player pool but were not included on the Nations Cup roster due to surgeries.

Colorado's college rugby scene has also demonstrated strength, with Colorado Mesa winning the Division II national championship and Colorado School of Mines winning the Division III title at this spring's Collegiate Rugby Championship. The Denver Sports Commission hosted World Rugby delegates in March 2025 as part of the ongoing site selection process for the 2031 tournament.

stories.headline: Snyder Fire Forces Dramatic Late-Night Evacuation of Rafting Party by Rail Truck
stories.summary:
A bachelor party and bachelorette party that had planned what participants described as a 'chill' rafting trip on a Colorado river were forced to evacuate in the early morning hours as the Snyder fire approached their location. The group rode out on rail trucks at approximately 2 a.m. to escape the advancing fire. The incident highlighted the unpredictable reach of Colorado's ongoing wildfire crisis into recreational areas during the Fourth of July holiday weekend.

The Snyder fire is one of five major wildfires burning across Colorado, which together have consumed more than 157,000 acres as of Friday afternoon. Extreme fire behavior driven by low humidity, high temperatures, and gusty winds has forced firefighting agencies to make rapid adjustments to evacuation orders and resource deployments across multiple counties.

joke.joke: You know what no one ever says? 'I wish this pizza had less cheese on it.'

topStory.headline: Trump Warns of 'Communist Menace' in Mount Rushmore Speech Marking U.S. 250th Anniversary
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump delivered a half-hour address Friday night at Mount Rushmore in Keystone, South Dakota, on the eve of the nation's 250th Independence Day anniversary, warning that the United States faces a resurgent 'communist menace' from within its borders. Speaking beneath the illuminated granite faces of George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Abraham Lincoln and Theodore Roosevelt, Trump declared that communism is 'the enemy of the Constitution' and a 'mortal threat to American liberty,' drawing comparisons to the September 11 attacks and World War II. Thousands of attendees who had won a lottery for entry watched the speech, which was followed by a fireworks display over the monument.

Trump framed the ideological threat in part around immigration, suggesting that certain undocumented arrivals and left-wing political figures embrace ideas 'totally opposed' to American values. 'You can be a communist, or you can be a patriot. You cannot be both,' he told the crowd. He also used the address to call for abolishing the Senate filibuster and passing the SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote and photo identification at the polls, arguing these measures were necessary for Republican success in the November midterm elections.

The speech came amid a string of recent primary victories by democratic socialist candidates in New York, Colorado and Texas, which Trump has publicly characterized as a leftist threat to American traditions. Trump also briefly addressed the ongoing U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, stating that Tehran is 'dying to settle' and that Washington had granted a pause in hostilities for the state funeral of Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, who was killed in a strike at the outset of the war. The address was delivered against a backdrop of voter concern over persistent inflation and elevated energy prices tied to that conflict.

Reaction to the speech fell along partisan lines. Republican strategist Eli Bremer told Al Jazeera that portions of the address were broadly patriotic enough to have been delivered by Ronald Reagan, though he acknowledged the divide between American left and right 'has really never been wider.' Democratic strategist Ameshia Cross argued Trump was reacting to a younger generation of Democrats winning elections across the country and that the speech reflected, in her words, 'a president who sees his grip on America steadily slipping away.' Cross also noted the address came days after the Supreme Court ruled against the administration in a case involving birthright citizenship.

Trump closed the speech by calling the anniversary 'the beginning of the golden age of America.' He made no mention of longstanding reports, dating to his first term, that he had expressed interest in adding his own face to the Mount Rushmore monument, a step that would require an act of Congress.
